Unexpected archaic introgression in ancient Eurasian genomes

Executive summary · AADR 1240K panel · generated 2026-06-29
Exploratory research. All findings are hypotheses requiring further validation, never definitive discoveries. No outlier is called a biological discovery without ruling out technical explanations and comparison with the literature.
15,443
ancient Eurasian genomes analysed
2.12%
mean Neanderthal ancestry
0
individuals beyond chance (after correction)
r = 0.88
agreement with published values
Bottom line. No ancient Eurasian individual in this panel carries Neanderthal or Denisovan ancestry that is statistically unexpected for their ancestry, geography and age after multiple-testing correction. The pipeline reproduces published estimates (including the famous Oase1 6–9%), so it would flag a genuine outlier if one existed — a rigorous negative result.

Validation against published papers

The estimator was validated internally (Phase 1: 7/7 gates — East-Asian excess, the Sardinian<French<Han<Papuan gradient, Neanderthals-as-test reading 97–99%, the Papuan Denisovan signal) and externally against the literature. Recomputing Neanderthal ancestry for 17 published anchors gives r = 0.88 vs published values, with 16/17 within range.

Decisive tests pass: Oase1 (Fu & Pääbo 2015, published 6–9%) is recovered as the single most elevated sample (5.1% standard, 9.8% damage-restricted — contamination removal recovers the true value); Ust'-Ishim 2.34% vs published 2.3%; Yoruba ≈ 0; Papuan Denisovan confirmed.

Dataset preparation (Phase 2)

Of 23,089 panel individuals, 15,443 retained Eurasian ancient genomes passed QC; 7,646 were excluded.

exclusion reasonn
present_day3,967
non_eurasian1,950
low_snps1,305
qc_assessment402
reference_or_archaic22

Archaic estimates & the noise floor (Phases 3–4)

Neanderthal ancestry (f4-ratio) and Neanderthal/Denisovan affinity were estimated for every genome with block-jackknife confidence intervals. The precision-weighted mean Neanderthal ancestry is 2.12% — the expected level for ancient Eurasians. Median usable SNPs per genome: 272,044; 9,252 samples are high-confidence (≥200k SNP, uncontaminated).

Per-genome standard error scales as 1/√SNPs (below), so single low-coverage genomes are intrinsically noisy — this drives the whole analysis toward precision weighting and the high-confidence subset.

Ancestry model (Phase 5)

Expected archaic ancestry must be conditioned on genetic ancestry, the dominant predictor. A panel-wide PCA places each genome on the West–East Eurasian cline (PC1, r=−0.67 with longitude), reproducing the published gradient East Asian > European > Near-Eastern/South-Asian Neanderthal ancestry.

Unexpected-individual detection (Phase 6)

For every genome we computed a standardized residual z = (observed − expected) / √(SE² + biological_scatter² + SE_expected²), where the expectation is the precision-weighted mean of its genetically/geographically/temporally nearest high-confidence neighbours.

Result: across 9,252 high-confidence tests, the max |z| is 3.90 (the multiple-testing threshold is z*=4.55); 0 individuals pass Bonferroni or FDR. The residual distribution is, if anything, narrower than the N(0,1) null — i.e. no individual carries archaic ancestry beyond chance once ancestry, geography and time are accounted for.

The nominal extremes (below) are the most-deviant individuals but are not significant; the strongest deficits are known ancestry outliers (recent African/Levantine admixture → less Neanderthal, as expected).

Robustness & sensitivity (Phase 9)

The conclusion was stress-tested under neighbour-count, reference-subsampling, tighter SNP-floor, and bootstrap perturbations. The null holds under all of them.

Phase 9 robustness — panel 1240k
================================================================
baseline K=80: max|z|=3.90  Bonferroni z*=4.55  #significant=0

(a) NULL STABILITY — #passing Bonferroni & max|z| under perturbation:
  perturbation                        #sig   maxZ  rho(z,base)  top20 kept
  K=40                                   0   3.89        0.992       17/20
  K=160                                  0   3.96        0.996       18/20
  reference 50% subsample (seed 0)       0   3.90        0.991       17/20
  reference 50% subsample (seed 1)       0   3.93        0.992       17/20
  reference 50% subsample (seed 2)       0   3.87        0.992       18/20
  tight floor >=400k SNP (n=2355)        0   3.97        0.981       15/20

(b) BOOTSTRAP of reference pool (B=100): distribution of max|z|
  observed max|z|=3.90; bootstrap max|z| mean=3.93 [3.79, 4.03]  (always < Bonferroni z*=4.55: 100%)

CONCLUSION: the null is robust — 0 samples pass Bonferroni/FDR under every
perturbation, and the bootstrap max|z| never reaches the threshold. The
residual ranking is itself STABLE (rho~0.99, 15-18/20 top candidates kept),
so the same individuals are consistently the most deviant — but their
deviations stay within the extreme order statistics of ~9,000 noisy tests
and the strongest are explained by known ancestry (African/Levantine
admixture -> less Neanderthal). No finding is retained as significant; the
stable top candidates are the only defensible targets for future deeper data.
